citation_sentences: The SEM micrographs of the 98% cold rolled and sample annealed at 750 °C for 5 min has been reported in Ref. [15]. The cold rolled sample (Ref. [15], Fig. 4c) shows heavily elongated and pancaked grains along the rolling directions. The presence of shear bands can be observed indicating non-uniform deformation. Shear bands were observed at an angle of \~40° with the RD. The average width of the deformed lamellae is $2 . 6 \pm 1 . 2 \mu \mathrm { m }$ . The sample annealed for 5 min (Ref. [15], Fig. 5c) and 60 min (Fig. 5) shows equiaxed and fully recrystallised grains. The grain/crystallite size has been reported in Table 2. Grain growth can be observed for the sample annealed for

associated with dark background. The size of the ordered phase has been determined to be $2 . 1 \pm 0 . 2$ nm. The size of the ordered phase measured from TEM is in correlation to that determined from SAXS studies (cf. Fig. 4).

It is well established that in polycrystalline materials, strengthening from (i) grain size and (ii) dislocations are the two potential strengthening mechanisms. In the present case, the presence of fine $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l }$ ordered phase has been established by Mössbauer spectroscopy, SAXS and TEM based studies. Based on the TEM and SAXS results, the ordered phase have been described to be of nano-sized dimensions with sizes ranging between 2.5 and 3.75 nm (Fig. 4). Therefore, the strengthening from nano-sized ordered phase can also be considered to be additional strengthening mechanism operating in the present case. Strengthening from ordered phase formation can be attributed to the difference in separation of the partial dislocations in ordered and disordered phase. This also results in the creation of an antiphase boundary between the two partial dislocations present in the ordered phase. The antiphase boundary is bounded by two dislocations (i) leading dislocation and (ii) trailing dislocation. The leading dislocation destroys the ordered structure whereas the trailing dislocation restores the ordered arrangement. This leads to the constraint on dislocations travelling in pairs thus restricting their mobility and leading to strengthening. In summary, the splitting of dislocations into super-partial dislocation in ordered phases leads to increase in flow stress.

citation_sentences: The distribution of ordered phase can also be observed from the dark field TEM micrograph shown in Fig. 6. The diffraction pattern shown in the inset shows the presence of (i) strong and (ii) weak spots (highlighted by yellow arrows). The strong spots are due to reflections from the matrix [011] zone axis whereas the weak spots correspond to superlattice peaks from ordered phase. Thus the diffraction pattern shows the existence of the ordered phase. The dark field image was obtained from the 00weak diffraction spots. citation_sentences: XRD scan of the Fe-6.8Al samples in the three conditions is shown in inset in Fig. 7a confirming the formation of ferrite phase. This is in corroboration to the results published by the same authors in Ref. [15]. Stabilization of the ferrite phase has been reported by the addition of Al to Fe by Rana et al. [3]. Rietveld refinement has been performed for the XRD profiles (inset Fig. 7a) using MAUD software [38] and the results presented in Table 3. For the determination of instrumental broadening, fitting of the XRD profile of Si standard sample was undertaken. It can be observed that the lattice micro-strain decreases after annealing from the cold rolled state which is due to recrystallization of the samples (Table 3). The decrease is sharp from the 98%CR sample to $7 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ 5 min annealed sample whereas it is shallow from 5 min to 60 min annealed sample as recrystallization is already completed upon annealing at for 5 min [15]. The (110) peak profile of the 98%CR sample shows a large amount of broadening as compared to that for the annealed samples which may be due to the presence of lattice micro-strain imparted during cold rolling. The broadening in XRD peak profile has been described in Ref. [39] to be caused by the presence of lattice imperfections. The two primary lattice imperfections are (i) crystallite size and (ii) lattice strain. The later manifests itself in the form of dislocations. Broadening due to dislocations becomes significant when dislocation density increases above $5 \times 1 0 ^ { 1 2 } \mathbf { m } ^ { - 2 } .$ . Fig. 7b–d shows the plots of second order (M2) and fourth order moment $\begin{array} { r l } { ( \mathbf { M } _ { 4 } / \mathbf { q } ^ { 2 } ) _ { : } } \end{array}$ , which were obtained after fitting (110) peak with Eq. (7) and Eq. (8) respectively. The dislocation density has been estimated and has been described in Fig. 7e. Fig. 7e shows that the evolution of dislocation density for the three sample conditions. It can be observed that dislocation density decreases rapidly showing a three order drop in magnitude upon annealing till 60 min at $7 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ as compared to the cold rolled sample. This decrease is due to the recrystallization of ferrite
